title: Imaging Surface Magnetization in Altermagnetic MnTe Films
authors: Ling-Jie Zhou, Senlei Li, Zi-Jie Yan, Yufei Zhao, Hongtao Rong, Zelong Xiong, Yiran Zhao, Pu Xiao, Lok Kan Lai, Hyeonhu Bae, Haoyu Liu, Chao-Xing Liu, Binghai Yan, Cui-Zu Chang, Hailong Wang, Chunhui Rita Du
abstract: Altermagnets with pronounced spin-splitting band structure, unconventional magnetic and crystal symmetries, and exotic magneto-transport properties have received immense interest in cutting-edge spintronics, materials science, and condensed matter physics research. Microscopic imaging of spontaneous magnetic domains and phases in altermagnets constitutes an important step for investigating their underlying material properties, mechanisms, and spin behaviors. Taking advantage of scanning-probe quantum microscopy, here we report nanoscale quantum sensing of a prototypical altermagnet candidate $α$-MnTe. We visualize evanescent magnetization and the associated magnetic domains in epitaxial MnTe films, which allows external magnetic fields to control the intrinsic altermagnetic order and configurations. By evaluating a series of MnTe films with different thicknesses down to the atomic scale, we further present evidence for the interfacial origin of the observed weak magnetization and show its correlation with the anomalous Hall effect in MnTe film. Our results advance the current understanding of emergent altermagnetism, providing insights into future material design of altermagnet-integrated spintronic devices.
summary: This paper uses quantum-sensing NV microscopy to image the magnetic domains of altermagnetic MnTe films at the nanoscale. It reveals that the weak out-of-plane magnetization is a surface-driven phenomenon linked to symmetry breaking at the interface. These findings provide a crucial experimental link between microscopic spin structure and macroscopic transport effects like the Anomalous Hall effect.
main_problem: The difficulty in experimentally imaging the nanoscale spin properties and verifying the origin of the predicted weak, uncompensated out-of-plane magnetization in altermagnetic MnTe.
main_result: The researchers successfully visualized evanescent magnetization and magnetic domains in MnTe films using NV microscopy, demonstrating that the magnetization is surface-dominated and correlates with the Anomalous Hall effect.
method: Scanning-probe nitrogen-vacancy (NV) microscopy was used for nanoscale magnetic imaging, complemented by ARPES for band structure characterization and first-principles calculations for thickness-dependent analysis.
model_or_system: Epitaxial alpha-MnTe thin films grown on InP(111)A substrates, characterized by an altermagnetic order with a Néel vector in the c-plane and a predicted weak out-of-plane magnetization due to symmetry breaking.
key_observables: Magnetic stray field maps, spin-splitting energy (delta), anomalous Hall resistance, and layer-resolved magnetic moments.
important_parameters: Temperature range from 2 K to 300 K, film thicknesses from 2 to 230 unit cells, and spin-splitting energy of approximately 280 meV.
main_assumptions: The observed magnetization is attributed to the breaking of mirror symmetry at the interface/surface.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Crystal structure, NV imaging schematic, and ARPES band dispersion; Fig 2: Magnetic stray field maps and histograms under different cooling fields; Fig 3: Thickness-dependent magnetization and first-principles calculations; Fig 4: Temperature-dependent NV imaging and AH resistance.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the altermagnetic system and imaging setup, presents the visualization of magnetic domains and their control via field cooling, analyzes the thickness dependence to identify the surface origin of magnetization, and concludes by correlating these findings with macroscopic transport properties.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1| Crystal structure and characterizations of epitaxial MnTe films. (a) Schematic of the crystal structure and antiferromagnetic order of MnTe. The Néel vector L = M1 − M2 is defined by two antiparallel magnetic moments (M1 and M2) of Mn atoms on neighboring sublattices. (b) ߶L denotes the angle between the Néel vector L and the [21ത1ത0] crystallographic axis in the c-plane of MnTe. (c) Theoretical prediction of the weak OOP magnetization ܯ௭ as a function of ߶L. Two groups of red and blue arrows represent the six in-plane easy axes of the Néel vector L, classified into two symmetry-related triplets. (a)-(c) Magnetic stray-field maps of a 40-UC-thick MnTe pattern measured under OOP-oriented magnetic cooling fields BFC = 0 (a), −1 T (b), and +1 T (c). The black dashed lines outline the boundary of the MnTe pattern. The scale bar is 500 nm. (d)-(f) Schematics of the possible configurations of the in-plane Néel vector L and the corresponding ܯ௭ in MnTe for BFC = 0 (d), −1 T (e), and +1 T (f). Color arrows within the hexagon denote the possible directions of the Néel vector L, and the corresponding directions of ܯ௭ are indicated by red and blue arrows on top.
